This chapter gives a working overview of From Prompts to Specifications: The First Build, focusing on the ideas a reader

Chapter 3 — From Prompts to Specificatio — "Add a page to my class website" is a request. A specification names the file, the structure, what it must not touch, an

beats.text: This chapter gives a working overview of From Prompts to Specifications: The First Build, focusing on the ideas a reader needs before moving to the next chapter. The chapter moves through Learning outcomes, Opening, The five elements, Prompt vs. specification: the worked table, and related ideas. Read it for the main argument, the vocabulary it introduces, and the practical judgment it asks you to develop. "Add a page to my class website" is a request.

segments.label: 01 / 13 · HOOK

title: OPENING
segments.body_html:
Not the whole class website. One page. The course's syllabus page — units, dates, topics, links to lesson materials. Sta

Opening — The teacher had two ways to ask.

beats.text: Not the whole class website. One page. The course's syllabus page , units, dates, topics, links to lesson materials. Static HTML. Linked from the home page's navigation. Nothing fancy. The first way: "Add a syllabus page to my class website."

segments.label: 02 / 13 · OPENING

title: THE FIVE
ELEMENTS.
segments.body_html: Operation.→Invariants.→Context.→Output format.→Negative constraint.

beats.text: The format is not a template; it is a set of categories that reliably matter. A specification is a prompt that contains decisions across all five. Operation. The specific verb and object. Create the file src/syllabus.html. Not "add a syllabus page" , that is a goal, not an operation. The operation names what file, what command, what change. If the operation cannot fit in one short clause, decompose into multiple specifications.

segments.label: 03 / 13 · THE FIVE ELEMENTS

title: PROMPT VS. SPECIFICATION:
THE WORKED TABLE
segments.body_html:
Operation — "Add a syllabus page"
Invariants — (implicit)
Context — (none)
Output format — (implicit"a page")

beats.text: Read the two columns. The request prompt lets Claude make four decisions for you: file path (default invented), CSS approach (default Tailwind probably), JavaScript (default included), styling (default inline). Each default is wrong for the class-website project. The specification removes all four decisions from Claude's scope; Claude generates against the constraints; the output fits the project on the first attempt. The 90 seconds of specification writing is the four decisions made by you instead of Claude.

segments.label: 04 / 13 · PROMPT VS. SPECIFICATION: THE

title: EXPLORE → PLAN →
IMPLEMENT → COMMIT.
segments.body_html: Phase 1 — Explore.→Phase 2 — Plan.→Phase 3 — Implement.→Phase 4 — Commit.

beats.text: The workflow that operationalizes the specification. Four phases. Each maps to a Claude Code affordance. Phase 1 , Explore. In plan mode (Shift+Tab twice). Claude reads files, identifies the relevant context, proposes the plan. You read the proposal. No file changes happen.

segments.label: 05 / 13 · EXPLORE → PLAN → IMPLEMENT → C

title: WHY PLAN
MODE MATTERS.
segments.body_html:
The cheapest defense against the dangerous middle is plan mode.

Why plan mode matters — Plan mode shows you what Claude would do before it does it. If Claude's plan reflects a wrong assumption — wrong file pa

beats.text: The cheapest defense against the dangerous middle is plan mode. Plan mode shows you what Claude would do before it does it. If Claude's plan reflects a wrong assumption , wrong file path, wrong CSS approach, wrong scope , the plan reveals it. You see the wrong assumption in 30 seconds of reading. You correct it (Ctrl+G; edit; save). The corrected plan executes. The wrong assumption never reaches the file system.

segments.label: 06 / 13 · WHY PLAN MODE MATTERS

segments.section: GIVE CLAUDE A WAY TO VERIFY IT
title: GIVE CLAUDE A WAY TO VERIFY
ITS OWN WORK.
segments.body_html:
A pattern from Anthropic's own engineering guidance: when your specification includes a way for Claude to check its work

Give Claude a way to verify its own work — For the syllabus page, the checks:

beats.text: A pattern from Anthropic's own engineering guidance: when your specification includes a way for Claude to check its work, Claude iterates against the check and produces dramatically better results. "After implementation, run npm run lint-html against src/syllabus.html and fix any reported errors before reporting done." "Take a screenshot of the page at 375px width and verify the table is not horizontally scrolling; if it is, fix the CSS."

segments.label: 07 / 13 · GIVE CLAUDE A WAY TO VERIFY IT

title: TEACHER BUILD:
THE FIRST DEPLOYABLE PAGE
segments.body_html:
·Compose the specification. Five elements. 100–200 words. For the first page of your site that does not yet exist.
·Run in plan mode. Shift+Tab twice. Submit the specification as your prompt. Read the plan.
Edit the plan. Ctrl+G. Make at least one changea path, a constraint, a step. Save. Return.
·Switch out of plan mode and execute. Watch the agentic loop run.
·Verify against the specification. Did Claude produce what the output-format element described? Did it respect the negati

beats.text: Apply the chapter's discipline to your class-website project. Compose the specification. Five elements. 100, 200 words. For the first page of your site that does not yet exist.

segments.label: 08 / 13 · TEACHER BUILD: THE FIRST DEPLO

title: CLASSROOM ACTIVITY:
STUDENTS BUILD THEIR FIRST DEPLOYABLE ARTIFACT
segments.body_html:
·All five elements present and specific.
·The task is executed through Explore → Plan → Implement → Commit.
·The plan is edited via Ctrl+G before approval (mandatory; the activity teaches the editing move).
·Commit message captures the specification's intent.

beats.text: Provide a starter project , a small website skeleton or a small data-processing script. Each student composes a five-element specification for one task: adding a page, adding a function, adding a feature. Their constraints: All five elements present and specific. The task is executed through Explore goes to Plan goes to Implement goes to Commit. The plan is edited via Ctrl+G before approval (mandatory; the activity teaches the editing move). Commit message captures the specification's intent.

segments.label: 09 / 13 · CLASSROOM ACTIVITY: STUDENTS B

title: COMMON MISCONCEPTIONS
segments.body_html: "Specifications are for big builds."→"More words = better specifications."→"I can just edit the generated code."→"Plan mode slows me down."→"Negative constraints feel paranoid."

beats.text: "Specifications are for big builds." Any operation that can fail silently deserves the format. The first page of the class website is small. The five-element specification still saves time on the first attempt. "More words = better specifications." No. Each element specific is what matters, not total length. A 30-word specification with each element specific beats a 200-word one full of generalities.

segments.label: 10 / 13 · COMMON MISCONCEPTIONS

title: AI WAYBACK
MACHINE.
segments.body_html:
George Pólya (1887–1985) — Hungarian-American mathematician whose How to Solve It (1945) formalized problem-solving as a

AI Wayback Machine — The Explore → Plan → Implement → Commit workflow is Pólya's sequence applied to Claude Code. Explore is Pólya's understa

beats.text: George Pólya (1887, 1985) , Hungarian-American mathematician whose How to Solve It (1945) formalized problem-solving as a four-stage sequence: understand the problem, devise a plan, carry out the plan, look back. Pólya's argument was that mathematical (and by extension, all technical) problem-solving was not the spontaneous insight popular culture imagined it to be , it was a teachable discipline with predictable stages. The teacher's job, Pólya argued, was to make the stages explicit so that the student could exercise each one deliberately rather than skip to the answer.

beats.text: George Pólya (1887, 1985) , Hungarian-American mathematician whose How to Solve It (1945) formalized problem-solving as a four-stage sequence: understand the problem, devise a plan, carry out the plan, look back. Pólya's argument was that mathematical (and by extension, all technical) problem-solving was not the spontaneous insight popular culture imagined it to be , it was a teachable discipline with predictable stages. The teacher's job, Pólya argued, was to make the stages explicit so that the student could exercise each one deliberately rather than skip to the answer.
